shortDefinition: AM cortisol (also called morning cortisol or serum cortisol) measures the body's main stress hormone at its daily peak and screens for adrenal and HPA axis problems.
fullDefinition:
## What AM cortisol measures

**AM cortisol** — also called **morning cortisol** or **serum cortisol** — is a blood test that measures circulating cortisol levels first thing in the morning, when the hormone naturally peaks. Cortisol is the body's primary stress hormone, produced by the adrenal glands under direction from the brain's hypothalamus and pituitary. Together, these structures form the [hpa-axis], the system that controls how you respond to stress, regulate blood sugar, and maintain energy through the day.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, AM cortisol is part of how we evaluate fatigue, weight changes, sleep disruption, and unexplained hormone symptoms — particularly when standard thyroid labs look normal but a woman still feels exhausted.

## Why it matters

Cortisol follows a natural diurnal rhythm: highest in the first 30–60 minutes after waking, then gradually declining through the day to its lowest point around midnight. This rhythm wakes you up, regulates blood sugar, modulates inflammation, and supports thyroid hormone conversion. When the rhythm is disrupted — by chronic stress, poor sleep, perimenopause, illness, overtraining, or undereating — the downstream effects are wide:

- Morning fatigue and afternoon crashes
- Weight gain (especially abdominal)
- Insomnia or 3 a.m. wake-ups
- Lowered immunity and slow healing
- Suppressed thyroid hormone activity
- Disrupted menstrual cycles and worsened perimenopause symptoms

Measuring AM cortisol on its own gives only one data point in a 24-hour pattern, which is why we often pair it with a [cortisol-rhythm] test or a [dutch-test] for a fuller picture.

## Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

**Conventional reference range:** 6–23 µg/dL when drawn between 6 a.m. and 10 a.m. The range varies meaningfully with the time of the draw — even an hour difference changes what 'normal' looks like.

**Functional/optimal target:** typically the upper third of the reference range when drawn within 30–60 minutes of waking — often around 14–18 µg/dL. A morning cortisol in the low single digits, even if technically 'in range,' often correlates with the symptoms women describe as 'adrenal fatigue.'

## What abnormal results suggest

**Low AM cortisol** can suggest [adrenal-insufficiency] (rare but serious — Addison's disease), pituitary dysfunction, long-term opioid or steroid use, or — far more commonly — HPA axis dysregulation from chronic stress, undereating, overtraining, perimenopause, or unmanaged hypothyroidism.

**High AM cortisol** can suggest acute stress, depression, [cushings-syndrome] (rare), or a disrupted sleep-wake cycle. Persistently high morning cortisol is also seen in chronic anxiety, blood sugar instability, and inflammatory disease.

Because cortisol is so dynamic, results are best interpreted in clinical context. If your morning cortisol looks off — or your symptoms suggest HPA axis trouble — work with a clinician who can pair labs with a thoughtful history and treat the underlying drivers.

commonQuestions.q: What time should I have my AM cortisol drawn?
commonQuestions.a: Ideally between 7 a.m. and 9 a.m., within 30–60 minutes of waking up. Cortisol drops sharply through the morning, so a 7 a.m. draw and a 10 a.m. draw can produce very different numbers — and labs do not always adjust the reference range to match. Tell the lab the exact time you woke up and the time of the draw, because that context is essential to interpretation. If your sleep schedule is non-standard (night shift, jet lag), let your clinician know so timing can be adjusted accordingly.

'Adrenal fatigue' is not a recognized medical diagnosis, but the cluster of symptoms it describes — chronic fatigue, low morning energy, salt cravings, postural lightheadedness, poor stress tolerance — is very real. The accurate term is **HPA axis dysregulation**, where the brain-adrenal communication system has been chronically stressed and stops producing cortisol on its normal rhythm. AM cortisol, paired with a 4-point salivary or DUTCH cortisol curve, can help map the dysregulation pattern and guide a recovery plan.

commonQuestions.q: How does cortisol affect thyroid function?
commonQuestions.a: Cortisol and thyroid hormone are deeply interdependent. Low cortisol slows the conversion of T4 into active T3, masks the symptoms of hypothyroidism, and can make thyroid medication feel ineffective or even agitating. Persistently high cortisol increases reverse T3, blocking T3 from binding to its receptor. This is why women starting thyroid medication sometimes feel worse if HPA axis dysregulation isn't addressed in parallel. At MTC we evaluate both systems together because treating thyroid in isolation often leaves a woman still exhausted.

seoTitle: AM Cortisol Test: Normal Range & What It Reveals | MTC
seoDescription: Morning cortisol reveals adrenal and HPA axis health. Learn the optimal range, draw timing, and what high or low results mean for your energy and hormones.
